source: VS Partners / Financial Times Global Seattle Symposium — Global technology investment: hype, hope, reality
summary: The metaverse is a real long-term trend — majority of people will spend majority of waking hours in virtual worlds
summary_extended: Baker argues that the metaverse represents a genuine long-term structural trend, predicting that a majority of people will eventually spend a majority of their waking hours in virtual worlds. He grounds this view in anticipated advances in computing power, software, and ultimately brain-computer interfaces, invoking the concept of 'reality privilege' — the idea that only those with access to exceptional physical-world experiences will prefer reality over virtual alternatives. His timeline is explicitly 50 years, making this a generational thesis rather than a near-term trade. Critically, Baker acknowledges significant uncertainty about which companies will lead this transition, stating directly 'I'm not sure who is going to usher it in,' which means no specific investment vehicle is implied with confidence. Given the 50-year horizon, the acknowledged uncertainty about winners, and the absence of any company-specific claims, no actionable ticker-level thesis can be derived from this view.
detail: Baker believes virtual reality will eventually be better than physical reality for almost everyone on the planet, driven by advances in computing, software, and eventually brain-computer interfaces. He references the concept of 'reality privilege.' Timeline is 50 years. He is uncertain who will usher it in.
confidence_evidence: Baker says 'I do think that is a real trend' but explicitly says 'I'm not sure who is going to usher it in.' Long 50-year horizon reduces near-term investment specificity.
quote_fragment: a majority of people will spend a majority of waking hours in virtual worlds
